summary_text:
S:
The patient reports pain to palpation of the lumbar spine. The patient reports pain in the left hand. The patient reports weakness in their right leg. The patient reports tingling. The patient reports a pop sound immediately after the injury. The patient reports a sweet tooth. The patient reports weight gain. The patient reports intermittent dizziness for one week, worse when standing. He denies chest pain, dyspnea, or syncope. The patient tried ice. The patient tried heat.

O:
The patient's vital signs are normal. The doctor appreciates a 2 out of 6 systolic ejection murmur. The patient reports decreased range of motion. The patient's right strength is 4 out of 5. The patient's left strength is 5 out of 5. The patient's hemoglobin a1c is 8. The patient's hemoglobin a1c was 8. The patient's hemoglobin a1c is 8. The patient's hemoglobin a1c is 8.

A:
The doctor assesses the patient has an acute lumbar strain. The patient has diabetes. The patient has back pain.

P:
The doctor prescribes meloxicam 15 milligrams once a day. The doctor orders physical therapy. The doctor orders an MRI. The doctor wants to see the patient in a couple weeks. The doctor ordered a refill of lisinopril 20 milligrams once a day. The doctor ordered a refill of lisinopril 20 milligrams once a day. The doctor prescribes meloxicam 15 milligrams once a day. The doctor orders metformin to 1000 milligrams twice daily. The doctor orders an hemoglobin a1c in a couple months.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Back pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

James Allen is a 57-year-old male with a past medical history significant for congestive heart failure and type 2 diabetes, who presents today with back pain.

The patient states he was golfing and hurt his back. This happened approximately 4 days ago when he felt a pop in his back. The patient notes that he immediately hit the ground trying to loosen up his back. He reports taking some Tylenol, ibuprofen, and using ice and heat which did not relieve the pain. By Monday morning it loosened up a little bit, but it is still pretty sore. He had some right lower extremity weakness and some drop foot that he felt that for a while, and it got a little better this morning but not much. He had surgery about 10 years ago in his lower back.

Regarding his congestive heart failure, he has been watching his diet and trying to exercise regularly. He is avoiding the salty foods and has been compliant with medications. He has gained a little weight over the summer but nothing more than 5 pounds. He has no problems lying flat to go to bed.

Regarding his diabetes, he is watching his blood sugars at home, not always consistently. He is still on his metformin.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

• Constitutional: Denies weight loss. Endorses weight gain.
• Musculoskeletal: Endorses back pain. Endorses right leg weakness and drop foot.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Cardiovascular: Grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur, stable.
• Musculoskeletal: Examination of his back reveals pain to palpation at L5. Decreased range of motion with flexion and extension. Positive straight leg raise. Strength is 4/5 on the right and 5/5 on the left.

Results:

X-ray lumbar spine, ordered and obtained in the office today, is unremarkable with good bony alignment.

Hemoglobin A1c is elevated at 8.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

James Allen is a 57-year-old male with a past medical history significant for congestive heart failure and type 2 diabetes, who presents today with back pain.

Acute lumbar strain.
• Medical Reasoning: The patient reports feeling a 'pop' and immediate pain while golfing approximately 4 days ago. He has tried Tylenol and ibuprofen without significant relief.
• Additional Testing: If he continues to have pain, we will obtain an MRI for further evaluation.
• Medical Treatment: Initiate meloxicam 15 mg once daily.
• Specialist Referrals: We will refer him to physical therapy to work on strengthening the area.

Diabetes type 2.
• Medical Reasoning: He has been compliant with metformin and dietary modifications, but his recent hemoglobin A1c was 8.
• Additional Testing: Repeat hemoglobin A1c in a couple of months.
• Medical Treatment: Increase metformin to 1000 mg twice daily.

Congestive heart failure.
• Medical Reasoning: He is doing well from this standpoint. He has been compliant with his medications, dietary modifications, and regular physical activity.
• Medical Treatment: Continue lisinopril 20 mg once daily and Lasix. Lisinopril was refilled today.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow-up in 2 weeks.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports pain to palpation of the lumbar spine.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Pain to palpation is a physical exam finding elicited by the clinician, not a typical patient-reported symptom; documenting it in Subjective could mislead a reader about the source of the finding.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports decreased range of motion.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is explicitly framed as a patient-reported limitation rather than an observed exam measurement, so it belongs in Subjective.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient's right strength is 4 out of 5.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is correctly in Objective as an exam finding, but the surrounding sentence structure mixes in a patient-reported item in the same section; organizationally it would be clearer to separate purely observed strength testing from subjective reports.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient's left strength is 5 out of 5.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Same as for right strength: objectively appropriate, but grouped with a subjective statement in the same line, which slightly blurs section organization.
